interactions.summary: Jiang shows Odysseus' worldview breaking at Troy: the Trojan Horse produces slaughter rather than justice, and the sight of a Trojan wife mourning her dead husband creates cognitive dissonance that becomes trauma and shame.

Odysseus is trapped in shame with Calypso, while Penelope and Telemachus are stuck at home. Athena's intervention frees him, but he returns disguised, unsure whether Penelope still loves him because trauma has cost him his capacity for love and imagination.
Penelope recognizes Odysseus not by public identity but by intimate memory: the brooch she gave him before Troy, which remains implanted in his mind as a symbol of everlasting love.
Penelope's bow contest lets Odysseus reveal himself. The bow symbolizes identity, and stringing it becomes the resurrection or re-stringing of his worldview as father, hero, and protector.
Jiang closes Homer by arguing that love heals war trauma and gives humans strength, courage, and power, then begins the Aeneid by introducing the Trojan Horse as Virgil's first anti-Greek object.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang; classroom questions are checks with no captured answer.

speaker_notes.note: Jiang asks if the class is clear and if there are questions, but no substantive student question is captured before he moves on.
The transcript says Iliad where context indicates the Aeneid when Jiang begins discussing the Trojan Horse from Virgil's text.

claims.claim: Odysseus' worldview collapses because he came to Troy for justice, legacy, and family reunion, but the Greek victory destroys families and becomes slaughter.
Jiang defines cognitive dissonance as conflict between worldview and reality.
Odysseus' PTSD appears as shame and avoidance: he is stuck with Calypso, crying on the beach, unable to go home because he cannot face his family.
Odysseus' absence traps the whole family in depression: Penelope cannot move on, Telemachus cannot inherit, and Odysseus cannot return.
Jiang argues that trauma has cost Odysseus his ability to fight, imagine, and love, so his first need on returning is to know whether Penelope still loves him.
The brooch lets Penelope recognize Odysseus because it is an intimate object only he would remember in detail.
The brooch symbolizes Odysseus' everlasting love for Penelope and his promise to return, even after the object itself has been lost.
The bow symbolizes Odysseus' identity; stringing it resurrects or strings together his worldview as father, hero, fighter for justice, and protector of family.
Jiang reads the Odyssey as the story of love healing trauma after war and bringing people back together.
claims.temporal_scope: Core Homer thesis in this lecture.

claims.claim: Jiang begins the Aeneid contrast by noting that Virgil opens with the Trojan Horse, where Greek eloquence makes a false story believable.

signature_moments.moment: Odysseus' worldview breaks because justice turns into slaughter.
signature_moments.source_phrase: It's not justice. It's just slaughter. It's just complete mayhem
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It shows trauma as a crisis of meaning, not only a memory of violence.

signature_moments.moment: Cognitive dissonance is the gap where worldview and reality stop matching.
signature_moments.source_phrase: when the worldview and the reality do not match
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is one of the lecture's portable psychological models.

signature_moments.moment: The postwar hero is crying on the beach, ashamed to go home.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It punctures the heroic victory narrative and makes trauma visible.

signature_moments.moment: Odysseus' whole family is stuck in depression.
signature_moments.source_phrase: this entire family, okay, is depressed
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The trauma is not private; it freezes a household and inheritance structure.

signature_moments.moment: The lost brooch survives because it is implanted in Odysseus' mind.
signature_moments.source_phrase: the brooch is implanted in his mind
signature_moments.why_it_matters: Memory, not possession, becomes the proof of love and identity.

signature_moments.moment: Stringing the bow strings Odysseus' worldview back together.
signature_moments.source_phrase: the resurrection, the stringing together of his worldview
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the clearest symbolic mechanism for love healing trauma after war.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Conflict between worldview and reality, used here to explain Odysseus' trauma after Troy.
Jiang applies PTSD to Odysseus' postwar shame, paralysis, and loss of capacity for love and imagination.
The intimate memory object that proves Odysseus' identity and symbolizes his promise and lasting love for Penelope.
The symbol of Odysseus' identity; stringing it resurrects his worldview.
The Odyssey's core message in Jiang's reading: love restores identity and reunites people after war.

uncertainty_notes.note: The transcript says Iliad when Jiang turns to the Aeneid and the Trojan Horse; use context to avoid confusing Homer and Virgil.
